coredata.dc:title: Solvothermal synthesis of functionalized carbon dots from amino acid as an eco-friendly corrosion inhibitor for copper in sulfuric acid solution
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yu
Zhang, Shengtao
Tan, Bochuan
Guo, Lei
Li, Hantang
coredata.dc:description: In this work, novel N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were synthesized from citric acid and l-serine. The results of fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) show that there are many unsaturated bonds and polar groups in the N-CDs. The inhibition performance of the concerned zero-dimensional nanomaterial for copper was investigated by electrochemical, combining FT-IR, XPS and Raman to investigate the corrosion products. Results indicate that the N-CDs were found to be effective inhibitor with the suppression efficiency as high as 98.5% means after immersed for 24 h, and they interacted with copper substrate by chemical & physical adsorption. Moreover, the related anticorrosion mechanism was explored and elucidated in detail. The purpose of this work is to explore eco-friendly and efficient corrosion inhibitor materials for metal protection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-CDs
Copper
Corrosion inhibitor
Raman
XPS

This work was supported by financially by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21878029 , and 21676035 ). Sail plan of Guangdong, China (No. 2015YT02D025).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Compendium diagram of the synthetic route of N-CDs.
TEM image of N-CDs.
FTIR information of citric acid, serine and N-CDs, respectively.
(a) FT-IR spectra of corrosion products on Cu substrates, (b) Raman information of corresponding Cu substrates studied.
(a) The characterization of N-CDs for XRD pattern, (b) High-resolution XPS spectra of N-CDs for Cu 2p (c), C 1s (d), O 1s (e), N 1s (f).

(a) The OCP-time curve of the copper electrode immersed in sulfuric acid solution containing different concentrations of N-CDs, (b) the polarization curve of copper in 0.5 M H2SO4 medium presence of various concentrations of N-CDs at 298 K.

(a) Nyquist plots of different concentrations of N-CDs of copper in 0.5 M H2SO4 medium, (b) the corresponding Bode plot, (c) fitting the blank EIS data with the equivalent circuit, (d) the equivalent circuit is used for the case where inhibitors are included.

(a, b) polarization result of copper electrode in 0.5 M sulfuric acid medium with and without N-CDs at different immersed time under 298 K, respectively. (c, d) Corresponding the EIS curve and Bode plot.
(a, b) polarization curve of unprotect and protect copper in 0.5 M sulfuric acid medium under different temperature, respectively.
(a) The EIS curve of unprotect and protect copper in 0.5 M H2SO4 environment at 298–318 K, (d) corresponding Bode plot.
The newly polished electrode surface (a), the copper electrode is immersed in 0.5 M H2SO4 with and without 200 mg/L N-CDs (b, c) for 12 h at 298 K.
(a) 3D AFM image and corresponding height profile of copper immersed in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution at 298 K for 12 h. (b) 3D AFM image and corresponding height profile of copper protected by N-CDs.
Linear fitting of the transition state equation of N-CDs protected and unprotected copper electrodes in 0.5 M sulfuric acid medium.
(a) Langmuir model and parameters of N-CDs adsorbed on copper surface in 0.5 M sulfuric acid medium at 298 K.
The anti-corrosion mechanism of N-CDs on copper substrate in acid medium is proposed.
Polarization parameters of protective copper electrodes with different concentrations of N-CDs in 0.5 M H2SO4 medium at 298 K.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a SD, standard deviation of the results of three independent experiments.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The result of electrochemical parameter fitting of EIS data is that copper in 0.5 M H2SO4 medium with and without various concentrations of N-CDs.
The results of electrochemical parameters fitted in EIS belong to N-CDs with a content of 200 mg/L in 0.5 M sulfuric acid medium with different adsorption times for copper.
Polarization parameters of copper in 0.5 M sulfuric acid medium with various concentrations of N-CDs at 298–308 K.
The electrochemical parameters of the EIS results of N-CDs for defended and undefended copper in 0.5 M H2SO4 medium at 298–318 K.
The corrosion kinetics data of Cu electrodes protected and unprotected by N-CDs in 0.5 M sulfuric acid are at 298–318 K.

head.ce:title: Solvothermal synthesis of functionalized carbon dots from amino acid as an eco-friendly corrosion inhibitor for copper in sulfuric acid solution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chongqing University, Chongqing 400044, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chongqing University, Chongqing 400044, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material and Chemical Engineering, Tongren University, Tongren 554300, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Material and Chemical Engineering, Tongren University, Tongren 554300, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Solvothermal synthesis of functionalized carbon dots from amino acid as an eco-friendly corrosion inhibitor for copper in sulfuric acid solution.

In this work, novel N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were synthesized from citric acid and l-serine. The results of fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) show that there are many unsaturated bonds and polar groups in the N-CDs. The inhibition performance of the concerned zero-dimensional nanomaterial for copper was investigated by electrochemical, combining FT-IR, XPS and Raman to investigate the corrosion products. Results indicate that the N-CDs were found to be effective inhibitor with the suppression efficiency as high as 98.5% means after immersed for 24 h, and they interacted with copper substrate by chemical & physical adsorption. Moreover, the related anticorrosion mechanism was explored and elucidated in detail. The purpose of this work is to explore eco-friendly and efficient corrosion inhibitor materials for metal protection.

ce:section.ce:para: Given its excellent thermal conductivity and malleability, copper and its alloys are widely used in electronic circuit and heating equipment. However, copper is easily corroded in humid environment, which will seriously damage the structure and performance of equipment [1,2]. In order to ensure surface with good solderability, conductivity and flatness during the manufacturing, pickling is one of the indispensable crafts to remove oxide on the surface, sulfuric acid was often introduced to industry owing to convenient transportation and less dosage [3–6]. Corrosion inhibitor was the most important anti-corrosion strategy ascribe simple operation and low cost, the application can effectively prevent the copper matrix from being severely corroded in sulfuric acid solution, it greatly improves the quality stability and has obvious economic value and social significance [7–9]. Lots of organic and inorganic corrosion inhibitors has been appraised, however, most of them are toxic, such as phosphate easily lead to eutrophication of the water environment, molybdate has low toxicity but is relatively expensive, chromate has low cost but is more toxic [10,11], so that the application of inorganic corrosion inhibitor was gradually restricted. Organic corrosion inhibitor molecules usually contain heteroatoms [12–14] (N, O, P and S, etc.), but most organic molecules still have high toxicity and high cost, restricted use by harsh corrosive media in practical industrial applications, it’s urgent need to develop high-efficiency corrosion inhibitors. Large-scale industry had led to water pollution and soil pollution are caused by heavy metals and organic pollutants, with the ascension of people’s awareness of environmental protection, the national sustainable development strategy puts forward new requirements for the development of corrosion inhibitors [15]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop low-toxic, non-polluting corrosion inhibitors to alleviate environmental problems, large molecules such as chitosan [16], starch, egg white, and NDA [17] have better film-forming properties than small molecules [18], even they are more environmentally friendly, but the inhibition performance cannot meet application requirements.

CDs are new type of zero-dimensional nanomaterials with good water solubility and abundant functional groups, due to its good biocompatibility, large specific surface area, and easy functionalization, it has developed rapidly in the fields of photoelectric catalysis and biochemistry [19–21]. Carbon nanomaterial has been found that huge potential application prospects in the field of metal anti-corrosion in recent years [22–26]. Based on the adsorption theories of organic corrosion inhibitors, theoretically, the thickness of the adsorption layer is at least several or tens of nanometers, and the large specific surface area and quantum size effect can contain more heteroatom groups [27,28], it is expected to explore novel high efficient corrosion inhibitors. The CDs synthesis strategy can be summarized in two strategies to adjust functional groups, one modus is CDs substances modify with functional groups [19,29], increasing the activity of CDs and metal adsorption, significantly change the self-corrosion potential of metals, thereby well prevent the contact between metal and corrosive medium. The another tactics is to adjust the doping elements, not only can maintain the advantages of the CDs, also heteroatoms can improve the internal electronic structure [30,31], bringing new electronic distribution states and characteristics. Liu et al [32] has prepared derivatives of ammonium citrate at different temperatures, the characterization shows that the N element is successfully doped into the CDs to form heterocycle. Three kinds of N-CDs showed good corrosion inhibition performance to carbon steel, the prepared N-CDs showed that the protective layer involved both physical adsorption and chemical adsorption. Yang et al [33] used imidazole ionic liquid to modify the prepared citric acid-based CDs as a green corrosion inhibitor, the results show that there is physical and chemical adsorption between the prepared corrosion inhibitor and the metal matrix, it has effective anti-corrosion performance for steel in neutral and acidic environment.

Amino acids with green, low-toxic and harmless are cheap and easy to obtain, abundant functional groups such as amino and hydroxyl are conducive to form cross-linked structure, easy to crack and carbonize at high temperature [31,34,35]. This study is dedicated to the potential corrosion inhibition performance of N-CDs for copper, citric acid as a carbon source, amino group of serine as a structural doping nitrogen source, and in-depth research on the protection mechanism of copper in H2SO4 medium. Transmission electron microscope (TEM), FT-IR and XRD explore possible internal structures. AC impedance and polarization to performe the anti-corrosion performance of N-CDs on copper, XPS, FT-IR and Raman were adopted to analysis corrosion products, combining atomic force microscope (AFM) and scanning electron microscope (SEM) to explore possible mechanisms, this exploration could provide a new field for the application of CDs.

l-serine (99%), citric acid (99 wt%), sulfuric acid (H2SO4 98 wt%), ethanol, pure copper block (10 × 10 × 10 mm3) were provided by local suppliers, and all raw materials are directly employed without optimization, the resistivity of ultra-pure water used in the process reached 18.25 megohms.

Fig. 1 displayed the synthesis summary of functionalized CDs, dissolved 0.38 g serine and 0.21 g citric acid in a 30 mL 50% ethanol aqueous solution by ultrasonic, then heat to 200 °C in 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave and keep it for 18 h, then naturally cool to room temperature to obtain a deep brown solution, then, the crude product is filtered through a 0.45 μm microporous membrane, and the filtrate is purified with dialysis bag for 24 h (molecular weight cut-off ~1000 Da). The purified solution was was further dried by rotary evaporator to remove most of the water, and then further freeze-dried to obtain N-CDs as black solids.

TEM (FEI Talos F200S) was introduce to observe the size distribution of N-CDs. Employed FT-IR (Nicolet iS50), Raman (LabRAM) and XPS (ESCALAB250Xi) and XRD (Ltd PANalytical X’Pert Powder) to gather structure information of N-CDs. Furthermore, collected FT-IR spectra from 400–4000 cm−1 were collected by KBr compression method, Raman semaphore was collected from 200 to 4000 cm−1, and XRD information was collected from 5 to 90°. XPS spectroscopy was collected spectrometer using an full-range, and collect XPS spectra using a full-range spectrometer is calibrated by the C 1s peak with 248.8 eV.

The pure copper block is encapsulated with epoxy resin, and only one side was exposed to the electrolyte for electrochemical testing. Before the test, the electrode is polished with 400–2000 mesh sandpaper, then ultrasonically cleaned with ultra-pure water and ethanol, and finally dried with cold air flow. 0.5 M H2SO4 solution was prepared as a solvent, and then N-CDs with concentration of 10–200 mg/L were prepared to electrolyte solution. In addition, each test uses a freshly prepared test solution, each group of laboratories three times to ensure reproducibility.

AFM (MFP-3D-BIO) and Field-SEM (JEOLJSM-7800F) were engaged to manifest the corrosion morphology of eroded and protected copper samples at 298 K. All copper samples were treated with 2000–7000 sandpaper and washed, and then the copper blocks were soaked in sulfuric acid solution without and with 200 mg/L N-CDs for 12 h. The information of corrosion products on the metal surface were collected and analyzed by spectroscopy (FT-IR, Raman) and energy spectroscopy (XPS).

The three-electrode system constructed by a copper electrode as the working electrode (WE), a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode (RE), and a platinum plate as the counter electrode (CE), besides, the RE is connected with a Lukin capillary to the electrolyte. The electrode system is immersed in the electrolyte for a period of time, and the open circuit potential (E ocp) is recorded to obtain a stable interface. Polarization and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were performed on the CHI760E electrochemical workstation, E ocp is applied as the initial voltage for the EIS test with a disturbance sinusoidal potential of 5 mV was executed from 100 kHz–0.01 Hz, employed Zsimpwin software to analyze EIS data. The polarization measurement is performed within the potential range of E ocp ± 250 mV at a scan rate of 2 mV s−1.

TEM was hired to analyze the morphology of N-CDs, disperse N-CDs on the ultrasonic carbon film for observation as shown in Fig. 2 . It can be clearly observed that the synthesized N-CDs have a uniform sphere size distribution of about 10 nm, and the TEM results indicate that N-CDs have been successfully obtained.

FT-IR information is used to investigate the functional groups of N-CDs as shown in Fig. 3 , comparing the absorption peak spectra of N-CDs with the information of the raw materials to confirm their transformation. In the spectrum of citric acid, the broad peak at 3420 cm−1 signifies the characteristic stretching vibration of OH and COOH, and the sharp peak near 1728 cm−1 is attributed to the strong absorbtion of CO [15,32]. The broad absorption peaks of serine at 3364 and 3385 cm−1 are due to OH and NH, the peak at 1600 and 1469 cm−1 attributed to stretch vibrations the CO and CN, respectively [33,36]. In the spectrum of N-CDs, the broad absorption peak at 3500–2900 cm−1 is affected by the multi-molecule association, it is the stretching vibration of OH and NH [37], the strong absorption peak at 1699 cm−1 corresponds to CO, the peak near 1456 cm−1 represents the existence of this CN [35,38], and there is a new and broader absorption peak near 575 cm−1, which indicates that N-CDs has produced a new ring structure. These hydrophilic functional groups indicate the stability and good solubility of CDs in aqueous systems.

The XRD information was shown in Fig. 5
a, indicating that graphene-like structure were formed in the obtained N-CDs. The camel peak shape correspondence with the peak of amorphous amorphous carbon, the diffraction peak is concentrated at the 2θ about 20°. Low graphitization and the peak shifts to the left was given the the introduction of N [39,40], the heteroatoms (O, N groups) in N-CDs leads to a wider carbon layer spacing compared to the graphene peak [32].

Fig. 4a shows that the FT-IR spectral band in the 400–4000 cm−1 region is derived from the corrosion products of the Cu surface modified by N-CDs to explore the possible mechanism. The wide range of 3600–3000 cm−1 attributable to the COOH,OH and NH groups of N-CDs, the new peak at 1078 cm−1 indicates the presence of sulfate, and the strong peak at 2361 cm−1 is the carbonic acid group [5,8]. It is obviously observed that the shift of the characteristic peaks of CO (1700 cm−1) and CN/CN (1435 cm−1) bonds corresponds to the appearance of chelating bonds between groups and Cu(I).

The Raman spectrum of protective copper is shown in Fig. 4b. The peak approximately locating at 2900 cm−1 are derived from the vibration of the N-CDs carbon-hydrogen bond, and the strong peak at 725 cm−1 was attributed to the aromatic ring vibration of the N-CDs [41]. The broad peaks are between 1500 and 2000 cm−1, originating from the vibrational absorption bones of sp 3 and sp 2 carbon atoms in graphene, indicating the successful doping of N in the graphene framework [42]. While the obvious signal of N-Cu can be observed in the range of 1000 cm−1, which is due to N-CDs forming chemical bond with copper and depositing on the surface. Furthermore, no significant CN chemical bonds was found at approximate 1200 cm−1 [43,44]. The results further confirmed that the chemical interaction between N-CDs and copper formed chelating bonds.

The particulars of XPS for copper samples decorated with N-CDs showed information corresponding to Cu 2p, C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, respectively. It can be observed that the XPS spectrum of Cu 2p can be attributed to Cu 2p1/2 (952.2 eV) and Cu 2p3/2 (932.1 eV) of Cu(I), no satellite peaks at approximately 943 eV and 963 eV manifesting that Cu (II) ramification were probed [45,46]. Fig. 5d shows the high resolution of C1s, the peaks at 284.7 eV, 285.4 eV, and 288.4 eV are attributed to the CC, CN, and CO components, respectively [36]. The peak of O 1s at approximate 531.2 eV corresponds to Cu2O, which are produced by the oxidation of copper and adventitious oxygen, the Cu2O peak in O 1s spectra further suggest that N-CDs restrain the oxidation of Cu+ into Cu2+, the peaks at 530.1 and 532.2 eV related to CO, and OH, respectively [12,47]. The N bonding energy in graphite as shown in the spectrum of N 1s in Fig. 5f. The chemical N 1s detected at a binding energy of 398.5 eV comes derived the N:Cu component, and the peak at 399.5 eV corresponds to the NH band, it means that the isolated electrons of pyridine-like N and pyrrole-like N can improve the strength of the coordination bond between the N-CDs and the metal interface [5,12,41]. XPS results show that the chemical coordination bond between N-CDs and copper is due to the chelation between the polar groups of N-CDs and Cu(I), no Cu(II) derivative was found on the copper surface, this can be speculated the transfer of Cu(I) to Cu(II) been restrain with N-CDs during the corrasion process, the results show that N-CDs deposited on copper by chemical adsorption.

The trend of the open circuit potential (OCP) mainly represents the change process of the electrode from unstable to stable, E OCP was recorded to understand the characteristics of the deposition process of N-CDs on copper surface. The OCP-time curves are shown in Fig. 6 a, compared with the blank solution, all E ocp curves migrated to the negative direction, and the presence of N-CDs makes the corrosion electric displacement to the cathode area, it means that N-CDs adsorb on the copper surface to prevent contact with water molecules, moerover, the deviation of E ocp value was less than 85 mV suggest N-CDs belong to mixed corrosion inhibitor [23]. As shown in Fig. 6b and Table 1 , it shows that that the current density of polarization curve with addition of N-CDs has an obvious suppression, which means that the cathode reaction (oxygen reduction) are suppressed. In addition, the current plateau clearly seen on the cathodic polarization curves attributable to the deposition of the nanoparticles on copper surface, while the desorption rate slows of anode branch was easily observed with addition of N-CDs, which indicates that the adaptability was formed between N-CDs and copper. These findings show that N-CDs can be believed to be mainly limit the cathodic reaction.

The potentiodynamic polarization was carried out to express the inhibitory performance of N-CDs for copper in 0.5 M sulfuric acid medium as shown in Fig. 6b. In addition, Tafel regions derived from the area near the E corr, correlation parameters are summarized in Table 1 including the corrosion potential (E corr), corrosion current density (i corr), slope of anode(β a) and cathode slope (β c), the suppression efficiency (η) was expressed as following [48]
(1)
η
( % )
=
i
corr , 0
-
i
corr
i
corr , 0
× 100 %
where i corr, 0 and i corr denote the current densities of the undefended and defended copper electrodes, respectively. Compared with the blank occasion, it can be seen that the adjunction of N-CDs will cause the negative shift of the E corr and significantly reduce the both i corr of the anode and cathode. According to reports [49], the cathodic oxygen reduction reaction and anodic corrasion process equations of copper in 0.5 M H2SO4 medium are as follows.
O 2
+ 4
H
+
+ 4
e
-
→ 2
H 2
O
C u ↔
Cu
ads
+
+
e
-
( f a s t )
Cu
ads
+
↔
Cu
2 +
+
e
-
( s l o w )

The conversion rate of Cu to Cu + ads is relatively fast, but Cu + ads is not easy to migrate into the electrolyte solution, which limits the generation of soluble Cu2+, and is considered to be the control step of the anode reaction, the combination of N-CDs and Cu(I) and Cu(0) means that the anode and the cathode are suppressed. In addition, the restricted corrosion rate depends on reducing the current density and changing overpotential, the value of η decreases from 51.26% when the concentration 10 mg/L to 77.3% at 50%, this may be due to the large-size N-CDs anchored on the copper surface to form a shielding layer then block the active sites. Moreover the desorption platform existing on the anode branch is observed, farther the desorption voltage is from the self-corrosion potential with the lower rate, which means that the N-CDs and copper anchoring are more stable. The results show that N-CDs can be considered as a highly effective hybrid corrosion inhibitor, which has the ability to significantly inhibit the cathodic process of copper.

EIS technology aimed to understand the anti-corrosion mechanism of N-CDs for copper in 0.5 M sulfuric acid medium, the obtained experimental data can be fitted with suitable equivalent circuit diagrams as shown in Fig. 7 c and d, so as to quantitatively analyze the adsorption behavior of corrosion inhibitor on the metal surface [49]. The capacitive circuit is determined by the double-layer capacitor in the high-frequency range, and the low-frequency capacitive circuit is allocated to the corrosion products of the membrane resistance. Constant phase components (CPE) are more in line with the actual solution/metal interface law than pure capacitors. Rs stand for the electrolyte resistance, Rf is resistance of the corrosion product layer, CPEf denote capacitance of the deposited film, and corresponding Nf represents the dispersion effect modulus, CPEdl express the double-layer capacitor, Ndl is the corresponding dispersion effect modulus, and Rct is the charge transfer resistance, and ω represents Warburg resistance caused by mass transfer at the electrode interface [50], χ 2 indicates that the fitting deviation refers to fitted and experimental data. Table 2 lists the electrochemical parameter values of the corresponding fitted results.

The modulus of CPE can be computed as Eq. (2), where j is the imaginary root, f max is the frequency at maximum imaginary part, Y 0 is the amplitude of the CPE, and n is the phase shift with the range of −1 to 1, which is the degree of dispersion index, the Y0 fitted value decrease with the addition of N-CDs, which could be ascribed to the nanoparticles anchored on copper surface form capacitive behavior. Two time constant displayed in corresponding Bode plots suggesting the suitable circuit diagram, the capacitance deviation of CPEdl can be counted according to the following program [51,52].
(2)
Z
CPE
=
Y
0
- 1
( j
ω
max
)
- n
(3)
C
dl
=
Y 0
( 2 π
f
z
max
)
n - 1

The suppressed efficiency (η) of N-CDs on copper in 0.5 M H2SO4 medium is evaluated by the following formula from the polarization resistance (Rp), where Rp represents the sum of Rct plus Rf,
(4)
R p
=
R f
+
R
ct
(5)
η
( % )
=
R p
-
R
p , 0
R
p , 0
× 100 %

Nyquist diagrams of copper electrodes protected by N-CDs in 0.5 M H2SO4 medium displayed in Fig. 7a and b, the diameter of the ring in the high-frequency region increases significantly with the increase of the N-CDs concentration, which is attributed to the capacitance response caused by the denser protective film adsorbed on the electrode surface. The W impedance disappears in the low-frequency region and instead appears capacitive arc, copper ions can only penetrate the film in a local area to form anode current, corresponds to the reduction of corrosion current density, this may be due to the protective layer reducing the mass transfer rate corresponding the metal dissolution process, which increases the charge transfer resistance process means diffusion slows down, It is speculated that the complex of N-CDs and copper atoms forms a stable potential barrier. The Bode plot shows that the impedance value increases within the tested frequency range, and the maximum value rised by one order of magnitude as the concentration of N-CDs increases from 10 mg/L to 200 mg/L. It maintains higher phase angle in a wider frequency range, and the maximum phase angle move to higher along with the addition of N-CDs increases. As the N-CDs concentration increases, the slope of the linear relationship between log|Z| and log| f | approaches −1, and the phase angle approaches −90°, which indicates typical capacitive behavior, it shows that N-CDs adsorbed on active sites on copper surface to form a dense film to inhibit the transport of corrosive substances.

The interaction was speculated based on spectrum and energy spectrum information combined with electrochemical behavior, N-CDs are rich in N and O groups and heterocyclic structures, so the lone pair of electrons on the outer copper layer can easily combine with N-CDs to form low-potential coordination bonds [53]. The formation mechanism of the acceptor–donor interaction between the nanomaterials and copper may be as follows [23].
Cu
ads
+
+ x N - C D s ↔
[ C u
( N - C D s )
x
]
ads
+
Cu + x N - C D s ↔ [ C u
( N - C D s )
x
]
where x represents the number of interactive between the nanoparticles and Cu atoms, [Cu(N-CDs)x] + ads and [Cu-(N-CDs)x] mean the main chelation product of the protective barrier on the copper surface is formed on the surface by chemical sedimentation. Meantime, it can be seen that capacitive appearance of the defended copper electrode is much higher than the unprotected copper as shown in Table 2, its impedance value is obviously greatly improved, indicating that N-CDs could greatly improve the corrosion resistance of copper. Obviously, the value of Rct increases and occupies a dominant position, while CPEf decreases with increasing concentration of N-CDs, it can be inferred the nanomaterials gradually fill the interface between the copper and solution. The anti-corrosion efficiency (η) value improved with the inhibitor from 86.3% at 10 mg/L to 93.6% at 200 mg/L. The results of AC impedance and polarization are basically similar, this indicates N-CDs can effectively stifle the corrosion of copper in sulfuric acid milieu.

The electrochemical behavior of copper electrode under different immersion times was to explore the stability in the N-CDs protection of copper corrosion process as shown in Fig. 8 , so as to determine when the corrosion rate is stable. In the blank solution, with the extension of the corrosion time, the cathodic corrosion current has obvious passivation, and the corresponding corrosion current density increases, and the corrosion degree of the electrode increases. In a shorter period of time, the corrosion products diffuse faster, because the corrosion film is incomplete and the corresponding resistance decreases, over time, the dense oxide film on the electrode surface increases resistance. As shown in Fig. 8c, the arc radius of the copper electrode protected by N-CDs increases greatly with the prolonged adsorption time, in the corresponding Bode diagram, it can be clearly observed that the capacitive behavior becomes more obvious, N-CDs replaces the moisture adsorbed on the metal surface, and the thickness of the layer becomes concentrated and more stable, so the capacitance of the composed interface layer is obviously smaller. It can be seen that the desorption behavior of the anode is obviously positively shifted with the time elapse in Fig. 8b, and the corrosion current density were greatly reduced, thereby greatly improving the protective performance. Table 3 lists the fitted parameters, the protective ability η of N-CDs against copper after 24 h of adsorption is as high as 98.5% compared with the corresponding blank solution, which means that N-CDs exhibits excellent inhibition performance for copper in 0.5 M sulfuric acid solution.

Electrochemistry was employed to quest the inhibition stability of N-CDs deposited on copper surface at 298–318 K, AC impedance and polarization were accepted to analyze the protective behavior of copper electrodes protected and unprotected by N-CDs in 0.5 M sulfuric acid medium, the fitted parameters was given in Table 4 and Table 5 . As shown in Fig. 9 , as the temperature increases, the desorption rate of N-CDs increases, and the passivation and shielding effects of the cathode branch weaken, so the corrosion rate of copper increases, meantime, the corresponding impedance (Fig. 10 ) becomes lower, which indicates that higher temperature will accelerate the diffusion rate of corrosion products. For the copper electrodes protected by N-CDs, the impedance value also shifts to lower with the increase in temperature, higher temperature aggravates the thermal movement of the nanoparticles, which leads to the adsorption layer unstable and destruction. Fig. 9b shows the Bode diagram, it is clearly observed the phase angle and capacitance characteristics scattered, means that N-CDs particles and corrosion products diffuse faster at higher temperatures. The elevated temperature weakens the adsorption film and corrosion resistance, it implies that the presence physical adsorption between N-CDs and copper since easily affected by temperature. In addition, the annual corrosion rate (Rcorr) is calculated as follows [9], where icorr is the corrosion current density, M stands for the metal molecular mass and d represent the density of pure copper.
(6)
R
corr
=
0.13 ∗
i
corr
∗ M
d

Fortunately, when N-CDs is added to 0.5 M sulfuric acid medium, even if the higher temperature will a intensify the corrosion of copper, it still shows better protection performance compared with the corresponding blank scenario, the η value drops from 93.6% at 298 K to 86.0% at 318 K, it is speculated that coordination of physical adsorption and chemical adsorption appear between N-CDs and copper, which means that N-CDs will still exhibit excellent inhibition performance by forming physicochemical adsorption on the copper surface.

The SEM photograph visually observes the texture of the metal surface to explore the protective effect of N-CDs for copper in 0.5 M sulfuric acid solution. Fig. 11 a dispaly a new polishing copper surface with uniform scratch, yet the unprotected copper surface can be clearly view that a large number of corrosion cracks in Fig. 11b, and the corrosion is quite serious. Fig. 11c demonstrate the copper surface defended by N-CDs showed a relatively smooth plane, which showed basically the same texture as the newly polished copper surface, indicating that the addition of N-CDs successfully slowed the corrosion of copper in sulfuric acid, which is also consistent with electrochemical results. Much reunited N-CDs parasitismed on the copper surface can be observed, it’s speculated that these nanoparticles self-aggregate eventually form a dense protective film on the surfce, effectively alleviating the corrosion of copper.

AFM presents microscopic information at the atomic level and is employed to observe morphology in the field of corrosion. Fig. 12 shows the AFM 3D map respectively showing the surface roughness of N-CDs for defended and undefended copper samples in 0.5 M H2SO4 medium for 12 h. As shown in Fig. 12a, the unprotected copper surface was corroded in blank solution has obviously more disorderly pits and porosity, and the corresponding height profile of the surface manifests a large fluctuation of about 200 nm, which indicates that the sample was severely corroded. When the inhibitor were added to the corrosive medium (Fig. 12c), the copper surface becomes relatively flat, and the corresponding height profile of the surface fluctuates reduce to about 50 nm, which means that N-CDs significantly reduces the degree of copper corrosion. The average roughness (R a) of the copper surface is reduced from 73.042 nm (corroded zones) to 22.146 nm (defended surface). The results are consistent with SEM and electrochemical results, further confirming that the presence of N-CDs can effectively shield the corrosion of copper by sulfuric acid solution.

Arrhenius and transient equation are introduced to research the adsorption kinetics of N-CDs on the copper/electrolyte interface. The thermodynamic parameters (activation energy Ea, activation enthalpy ΔHa, activation entropy ΔSa) have been calculated and listed in Table 6 , ΔHa and ΔSa are respectively derived from the slope and intercept of the transient equation, that is, the regression between linear lnRcorr and 1/T (Fig. 13 ), where Rcorr represents the corrosion rate with the unit is mpy as shown in Table 4, A is the Arrhenius exponent factor, R is the gas constant, T is the absolute temperature, h is the Planck constant, and N is the Avogadro number. The expressions of Arrhenius equation and transient equation are as follows [9,54]:
(7)
l n
R
corr
= l n A -
E a
RT
(8)
R
corr
=
RT
Nh
e x p
(
Δ
S a
R
)
e x p
(
- Δ
H a
RT
)

The value of Ea measured in the medium presence N-CDs is higher than the value in the absence, indicating that the lower energy barrier in the first stage of the adsorption process means electrostatic adsorption, which is beneficial to promote the interaction between Cu+ and the inhibitor. The positive ΔHa reflects the endothermic nature of the copper dissolution process, due to the adsorption of N-CDs on the copper surface preventing charge transfer, which means that the dissolution of copper is slow and difficult. Since the molecular weight and size of the nanoparticles are much larger than water molecules, the more positive of ΔS° means that the entropy of solute adsorption and solvent desorption increases, thereby reducing the disorder of the metal/solution interface.

Electrochemical and morphological illustrate that N-CDs can suppress the corrosion of copper in acid solution, analyzing the adsorption isotherm of N-CDs with copper surface to understand their reciprocity. EIS data fits the Langmuir isotherm model, and its linear regression coefficient (R2) is 0.9999 (Fig. 14 ), the results show a good linear relationship. The calculation models of related important parameters are shown in follows [2,55].
(9)
θ =
η 100
(10)
θ
1 - θ
=
K
ads
C
(11)
K
ads
=
1
C
H 2
O
e x p
(
- Δ
G
ads
0
RT
)
where Kads is the adsorption equilibrium constant derived from the intercept of the Eq. (10), G0 adss is the standard free energy of adsorption, CH2O is the density of the electrolyte solution close to water (1000 g/L), R is the molar gas constant, and T is the experimental temperature (K). K ads and △G0 adss values are calculated as shown in Fig. 14, the consensus were recognized that the G0 ads values of −40 kJ/mol or more negative owning to chemisorption that charge sharing or transfer, while the values of −20 kJ/mol or less negative can be considered as physisorption because of the electrostatic interaction [56]. As a result, the K ads is high to 599 L/g and △G0 adss low to −27.25 kJ/mol, means strong chemisorption interaction between copper and the inhibitor.

This work designed a new zero-dimensional N-CDs from amino acids as raw materials, it was first invited as a corrosion inhibitor for copper protection in 0.5 M sulfuric acid environment. The results of TEM indicate that the particle size of N-CDs is uniformly distributed around 10 nm, and the structure information shows that they are rich in hydrophilic groups, unsaturated bonds and heterocyclic rings, these characteristics are conducive to the adsorption of nanomaterials on the copper surface. As shown in Fig. 15 , the electrochemical performance shows that N-CDs can be considered as a cathodic corrosion inhibitor, and N-CDs adsorb on the copper surface to form a stable protective layer, which prevents the copper and corrosive media from interacting with erosion, the inhibition efficiency increases up to 98.5% with the increased concentration. Furthermore, the protective effect is still better at higher temperatures, and the electrochemical data reveals the existence of physical adsorption. Applicability between N-CDs and copper electrodes comply with the Langmuir model, combined with spectral and energy spectrum information, it can be inferred that the interaction is mainly chemical adsorption. The observation of surface morphology provides strong endorsement for the electrochemical results and further confirms that N-CDs can be used as an efficient corrosion inhibitor for copper in 0.5 M sulfuric acid environment.

Yu Zhang: Writing - original draft, Investigation, Software, Data curation. Shengtao Zhang: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Project administration. Bochuan Tan: Writing - review & editing, Methodology. Lei Guo: Writing - review & editing. Hantang Li: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by financially by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21878029, and 21676035). Sail plan of Guangdong, China (No. 2015YT02D025).
